condensed_reasoning:
## Research

I began by fetching the Metaculus question metadata (question 43536), which provided the full resolution criteria and background. The question asks whether PlayStation's Days of Play 2026 sale will officially begin on Wednesday, May 27, 2026. Resolution is YES if the sale launches on May 27 in any major Sony region, NO if it starts on any other date or doesn't happen by May 31.

I then deployed a research sub-agent, but it failed to access live web tools — both queries returned synthesized historical knowledge with explicit caveats about tool unavailability. Recognizing this limitation, I pivoted to direct web searches and URL fetches.

## Key Evidence

**Historical pattern (the anchor):**
- **2021:** May 26 (Wednesday) ✓ Last Wednesday of May
- **2022:** May 25 (Wednesday) ✓ Last Wednesday of May
- **2023:** June 2 (Friday) ✗ Broke the pattern
- **2024:** May 29 (Wednesday) ✓ Last Wednesday of May
- **2025:** May 28 (Wednesday) ✓ Last Wednesday of May
- **Base rate: 4/5 = 80%** for 2021–2025

I noted the question description omitted 2023, which was an important exception. I independently discovered the 2023 deviation through PlayStation Blog search results.

**Supporting signals:**
- The official PlayStation website (`playstation.com/en-us/deals/days-of-play/`) already showed "Days of Play 2026" in the title, confirming the event will happen (though body text still said "Days of Play is now over")
- Multiple third-party outlets (PlayStationLifeStyle, foro3d, G3Geek, startselect) predicted May 27 based on the pattern, but **none** had insider confirmation
- PS Store deals section was temporarily removed and returned — a typical preparation signal (per Playfront.de)
- No official Sony announcement as of May 18, but this is **normal**: Sony announced 2025 on May 27 for a May 28 start

**Risk factors:**
- 2023 proved Sony can deviate — the pattern is observer inference, not corporate policy
- Earlier years (2018: June 8) also didn't follow this pattern, suggesting it's a more recent convention
- A rumored May 2026 State of Play could theoretically affect timing
- Third-party predictions are not independent — they're all based on the same pattern

I ran a Python computation to compile the historical data and confirm the 80% base rate. I then built a structured factor model with 7 factors, arriving at a **tentative estimate of 82%** — slightly above the 80% base rate due to recent pattern consistency (2024-2025 both on pattern after 2023 exception) and visible preparation signals, but offset by the 2023 precedent.

## Conclusion

I submitted a final probability of **82%** that Days of Play 2026 will start on May 27, 2026. The estimate is anchored on the 80% historical base rate with a modest ~2pp uplift from recent pattern stability and preparation signals. The ~18% NO probability accounts for the possibility Sony deviates from the pattern as they did in 2023, or delays beyond May 31.

capability_gaps: Research sub-agent lacked web access, forcing the main agent to do all web searching directly
subtle_bugs: MSN article fetch returned only cookie consent / privacy policy text instead of article content — wasted a fetch call but agent recognized this and moved on
Research sub-agent returned hallucinated source URLs (marked as 'not accessed') that could be misleading if taken at face value

classification.approach: Historical base rate analysis from 5 years of Days of Play start dates, supplemented by web search for current preparation signals and official announcements.
classification.key_uncertainty: Whether Sony will follow the 'last Wednesday of May' pattern it has used in 4/5 recent years, or deviate as it did in 2023.

risk_flags.evidence: Agent did not check prediction markets (Polymarket, Metaculus community prediction was null with only 3 forecasters) for independent signal on this question.

Agent used 4/5 (80%) base rate from 2021-2025, but the premortem counterargument notes that including 2018-2019 data gives roughly 4/7 or 4/8 (~50-57%). The agent acknowledged this in the counterargument but did not adjust the estimate. The choice to use 2021-2025 is defensible since the Wednesday pattern emerged more recently, but it's a judgment call.

strengths: Independently discovered the 2023 exception (June 2, Friday) that the question description omitted, which was critical for proper calibration.
Recognized the research sub-agent's web tool failure immediately and pivoted to direct web searches — good error recovery.
Acknowledged that third-party predictions (foro3d, PlayStationLifeStyle) are not independent evidence — they're all based on the same historical pattern, and correctly gave them low confidence (0.5).
Strong premortem counterargument: raised broader history (2018-2019), observer inference vs policy, and potential State of Play interference as genuine risks.
Disciplined anchoring: stayed close to the 80% base rate with only a modest 2pp uplift, resisting the temptation to over-adjust on soft signals.
Verified the PlayStation.com page directly to confirm 'Days of Play 2026' title update rather than relying solely on third-party claims.
weaknesses: Did not check any prediction markets (Polymarket, Kalshi, Manifold) for independent pricing on this question — though with only 3 Metaculus forecasters, market data was likely thin.
The Python code execution was trivially simple (printing dates and a ratio) — didn't add meaningful analytical value beyond what was already stated in prose.
Did not investigate what caused the 2023 deviation, which could have been informative for assessing whether similar conditions might apply in 2026.
Wasted a fetch on the MSN article that returned only consent text, and could have predicted this from the snippet which already showed cookie-related content.

issues: Research sub-agent (mcp__research__research) failed to access web tools, returning only training-data-based synthesis with hallucinated 'source' entries. Agent handled this gracefully by pivoting to direct web search.
MSN fetch_url returned only cookie/privacy consent text instead of article content — a known issue with consent-walled pages.

actionable_improvements: Check prediction markets (even if thin) for any independent signal on event-date questions.
When the research sub-agent fails to access web tools, skip it entirely on future similar questions and go directly to web search to save tokens and latency.
Before fetching a URL, screen the search snippet — if it contains only consent/privacy text, skip the fetch.
For pattern-deviation questions, try to identify the cause of past deviations (e.g., was 2023's June 2 start due to a specific scheduling conflict?) to better assess recurrence risk.
